title: SMR's OTHER Fatal Flaw: Far too many designs—27+ in U.S.—mean 'virtually all SMR companies will fail and costs will remain very high for a long time' as I explained in a major UPenn report | Joe Romm
text:
SMR's OTHER Fatal Flaw: Far too many designs—27+ in U.S.—mean 'virtually all SMR companies will fail and costs will remain very high for a long time' as I explained in a major UPenn report Please repost. But if you'd like to triple your followers and 10x your engagement, listen to my new podcast (with my hilariously skeptical daughter) on the secrets of viral storytelling. It's already 7th in Apple's Music category here: https://lnkd.in/e9BFZ-Wh Also on Spotify: https://lnkd.in/e5eZykeV So, the main fatal flaw of smaller reactors (300 MW or less) is that they inherently cost much more per MW than the already wildly expensive large ones (1000+ MW) that comprise 99% of global power generation. The only U.S. nuclear plant built this century, the twin Vogtle reactors in Georgia, cost over $35 billion for 2.2 gigawatts, which is over $15 million per megawatt. Yet a 2023 analysis concluded that “if the costs of new nuclear end up being much higher” than $6.2 million per megawatt, “new nuclear appears unlikely to play much of a role, if any, in the U.S. power sector.” But the Dept. of Energy’s own 2024 “Pathways to Commercial Liftoff: Advanced Nuclear” report modeled a median cost per MW over 50% higher for SMRs than for large reactors! That "REPORT MAKES CLEAR WE WOULDN’T PURSUE COUNTLESS SMR DESIGNS IF WE WERE SERIOUS ABOUT NUCLEAR. SAVINGS FROM MODULARITY REQUIRE MASS-PRODUCING ONE OR, AT MOST, TWO DESIGNS," as I wrote in an April analysis for UPenn. "The current U.S. strategy means virtually all SMR companies will fail, and costs will remain very high for a long time." https://lnkd.in/e62d5Q-6 Yet, the latest edition of the OECD Nuclear Energy Agency's SMR Dashboard reports that of the 127 total SMR designs, 51 SMR DESIGNS GLOBALLY ARE INVOLVED IN PRE-LICENSING OR LICENSING—OF WHICH 27 ARE IN THE U.S. But there is no reason to believe that the economies of standardization if they actually do manifest, would be large enough to overcome both the diseconomies of shrinkage and the inherently high cost of nuclear plants. Yet that is exactly what would be needed to create a successful commercial SMR to compete in the post-2040 electricity market. So FIRST we must go through an extensive process of winnowing out the inevitable 95+% losers. SECOND, we'll have to see if 1 or 2 designs emerge that can actually compete in the increasingly brutal market for cheap and/or dispatchable power. Remember solar, wind, and especially batteries keep getting cheaper and cheaper. Plus we're seeing advances in emerging competitors to nuclear power, such as enhanced geothermal systems. So, SMRs, require all 4 of these: a lot of time, a major breakthrough, faltering competitors, and someone with deep pockets (to build a manufacturing plant that might be a total failure). Maybe one or two might be scalable by 2050. I wouldn't bet on that, though. Renewables and storage are here now. Let's go!
Even if SMRs can manage to reduce energy costs as well be used for large scale energy production ( unlikely, although theoretically possible), it would take 20-30 years to reach those goals. Planet earth does not have that long. The effects of climate change are already noticeable right now (and you do not need a scientific paper to see that the earth is warming). The target of 1.5deg yearly global warming, as specified by the Paris agreement, has already been reached in 2024.
A similar opinion: Professor M.V. Ramana (nuclear expert at UBC) writes in "Small Modular and Advanced Nuclear Reactors: A Reality Check" (2021) https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9374057 "Sustained learning would also require just one or two standard reactor designs to be chosen for build-out in those large quantities. However, as mentioned earlier, roughly six dozen SMR designs are in various stages of development in multiple countries [1]. It is very unlikely that one, or even a few designs, will be chosen in a coordinated fashion by different countries and private entities, discarding the vast majority of designs that are currently being invested in."
